coredata.dc:title: Carbon spherical shells in a flexible photoelectrochemical sensor to determine hydroquinone in tap water
coredata.dc:creator: Martoni, Lucas V.L.
Gomes, Nathalia O.
Prado, Thiago M.
Calegaro, Marcelo L.
Oliveira Jr., Osvaldo N.
Machado, Sergio A.S.
Raymundo-Pereira, Paulo A.
coredata.dc:description: Analytical tools are essential to monitor anthropogenic contamination of natural resources and prevent health risks to humans and other living beings, which should be low-cost, with fast response and high sensitivity. In this work we developed a photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor to determine hydroquinone in tap water using a screen-printed carbon electrode functionalized with carbon spherical shells (CSS) synthesized using the hydrothermal method. The CSS had an average size of 150 ± 30 nm, and a band gap of 1.3 eV estimated with a Tauc plot using data from diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. With CSS in the sensing layer, the photoelectrochemical sensor could determine hydroquinone within a linear range from 3.0 to 23.0 µmol L–1, sensitivity 0.07 µA µmol–1 L and limit of detection (LOD) 2.7 µmol L–1 (signal-to-noise ratio of 3). The PEC sensor was reproducible, selective and amenable to use in complex matrices, such as tap water. CSS are advantageous as they can be obtained at low cost with green methodologies for mass production, and then be employed in PEC sensors for routine analysis of emerging pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon spherical shells
Screen-printed carbon electrodes
Hydroquinone
Photoelectrochemical sensor
Water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical performance of the PEC sensor
Scanning electronic microscopy of PEC sensor

Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financed in part by Coordena??o de Aperfei?oamento de Pessoal de N?vel Superior ? Brasil (CAPES) ? Finance Code 001. The authors are thankful to S?o Paulo Research Foundation - FAPESP (grants 2020/09587?8, 2018/22214?6, 2019/01777?5 and 2016/01919?6), National Council of Technological and Scientific Development - CNPq (grants 140408/2019?3, 164569/2020?0, 151200/2022?0 and 423952/2018?8). The authors are especially grateful to Mr. Marcio de Paula (CAQI/IQSC/USP) for the SEM images.

This study was financed in part by Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior – Brasil (CAPES) – Finance Code 001 . The authors are thankful to São Paulo Research Foundation - FAPESP (grants 2020/09587–8 , 2018/22214–6 , 2019/01777–5 and 2016/01919–6 ), National Council of Technological and Scientific Development - CNPq (grants 140408/2019–3 , 164569/2020–0 , 151200/2022–0 and 423952/2018–8 ). The authors are especially grateful to Mr. Marcio de Paula (CAQI/IQSC/USP) for the SEM images.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic representation of the complete system to determine hydroquinone using the PEC sensor. (B) Schematics of the photo-generated electron-hole transfer mechanism for CSS at the electrode. (C) Tauc plot for carbon spherical shells.

(A) XPS survey scans of CSS. (B) C 1s XPS spectrum and (C) O 1s XPS spectrum of CSS. (D) Nyquist diagrams for flexible SPCE (a) and flexible SPCE modified with CSS (b). The measurements were performed in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl solution (pH 7.0) containing 5.0 × 10−3 mol L−1 of redox probe [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−. Inset: Equivalent circuit model for fitting the data.

(A) Photocurrent responses for electrodes functionalized with CSS suspensions: 2.5, 5.0, 7.5, 10 mg mL–1 and bare SPCE; (B) current vs. concentration of CSS suspension (experimental conditions: E = 0.1 V, UV LED and PBS pH = 7.08). (C) Photocurrent for PEC sensors with white LED and UV LED (experimental conditions: E = 0.1 V, PBS pH = 7.08 and 5.0 mg mL–1 CSS). (D) Photocurrent for potentials 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 and 0.4 V; (E) current vs. potential (experimental conditions: PBS pH = 7.08, UV LED and 5.0 mg mL–1 CSS). (F) Photocurrent for PEC sensors using 0.1 mol L–1 PBS, Na2SO4, Na2C2O4 and KCl as supporting electrolytes (experimental conditions: E = 0.2 V, UV LED and 5.0 mg mL–1 CSS). All experiments were conducted with HQ concentration fixed at 100 µmol L–1.

(A) Photocurrent for PEC sensors to determine HQ at increasing concentrations between 3.0 and 23.0 µmol L–1 in 0.1 mol L–1 Na2C2O4 pH = 7.18. Experimental conditions: E = 0.2 V, UV LED, CSS suspension = 5.0 mg mL–1. (B) Corresponding calibration curve (n = 3): ∆I [µA] = 0.069 ( ± 0.004) Chydroquinone [µmol L–1] – 0.07 ( ± 0.04), R2 = 0.990.

(A) Photocurrent response for the reproducibility study intraday (n = 5); (B) inter-day (n = 3). Conditions: 13 µmol L–1 HQ in 0.1 mol L–1 Na2C2O4, pH = 7.18. (C) Repeatability study. The concentration for hydroquinone and interferents was 13.0 µmol L–1. Experimental conditions: concentration of CSS suspension 5.0 mg mL–1, 0.1 mol L–1 Na2C2O4 pH = 7.18, UV LED.

Interference study with ascorbic acid (vitamin C), hydrochlorothiazide, L -proline, β-estradiol, thiamine (vitamin B1), diuron and pentachlorophenol. The concentration for hydroquinone and interferents was 13.0 µmol L–1. Experimental conditions: concentration of CSS suspension 5.0 mg mL–1, 0.1 mol L–1 Na2C2O4 pH = 7.18, UV LED.

(A) SEM images of CSS; (B) particle size distribution histogram; (C) EDX spectra for CSS surface; (D) SEM image of bare SPCE; (E) Image of SPCE functionalized with 5.0 mg mL–1 CSS; (F) HR-TEM images of the CSS.
Comparison of band gaps for several carbonaceous materials.

row.entry: The band gap varied with the oxygen amount, with 0.78 being one of the estimated values.
The band gap varies with the size, shape and presence of functional groups.
Band gap can be changed through doping or copolymerization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of electrochemical and PEC methods for HQ determination.

row.entry: Skin whitening cream and river water
PEC sensor – CA – connected to FIA system

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a: gold electrode modified with N-doped reduced graphene oxide and ZnO.
b: SPE modified with multiwalled carbon nanotubes and gold nanoparticles.
c: ITO modified with BIPO4 and graphene quantum dot.
d: film of cooper hexacyanoferrate (CuHCF) deposited on silicon electrode coated by a platinum (Pt) layer.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery values for HQ determination in tap water samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon spherical shells in a flexible photoelectrochemical sensor to determine hydroquinone in tap water

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: São Carlos Institute of Chemistry, University of São Paulo

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: São Carlos Institute of Physics, University of São Paulo

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Analytical tools are essential to monitor anthropogenic contamination of natural resources and prevent health risks to humans and other living beings, which should be low-cost, with fast response and high sensitivity. In this work we developed a photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor to determine hydroquinone in tap water using a screen-printed carbon electrode functionalized with carbon spherical shells (CSS) synthesized using the hydrothermal method. The CSS had an average size of 150 ± 30 nm, and a band gap of 1.3 eV estimated with a Tauc plot using data from diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. With CSS in the sensing layer, the photoelectrochemical sensor could determine hydroquinone within a linear range from 3.0 to 23.0 µmol L–1, sensitivity 0.07 µA µmol–1 L and limit of detection (LOD) 2.7 µmol L–1 (signal-to-noise ratio of 3). The PEC sensor was reproducible, selective and amenable to use in complex matrices, such as tap water. CSS are advantageous as they can be obtained at low cost with green methodologies for mass production, and then be employed in PEC sensors for routine analysis of emerging pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: The anthropogenic contamination of natural resources with chemical compounds from industrial waste and agricultural activity affects the quality of air, soil and water. Damage to human health and other living beings may occur, in addition to disequilibrium in ecosystems [1–6]. In water sources, for instance, one of the main pollutants is the benzene metabolite hydroquinone (bezene-1,4-diol, HQ) [7] used in pharmaceuticals, dyes, plastics, pesticides, paper and cellulose industry, being discarded via industrial waste [7]. HQ is classified by the European Chemical Agency (ECHA) as corrosive (GHS05), serious hazard for human health (GHS08), health hazard (GHS07) and hazardous to the environment (GHS09) [8]. Health risks caused by HQ include skin irritation and other dermatological diseases, allergy, eye injuries, gastrointestinal symptoms and DNA damage [9–13]. HQ toxicity to aquatic life was also reported, with harmful effects to algae, bacteria, fishes and mammalians [7,14,15]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), HQ is toxic to several aquatic organisms even at concentrations below 1 mg L–1 (9.1 µmol L–1) [13]. Thus, monitoring and controlling HQ in water samples is essential. The most used methods to determine HQ are chromatography [16–18] and UV-Vis spectroscopy [19], which are time consuming and demand solvents for extraction and cleaning steps [20], in addition to requiring well-trained technicians [21]. Furthermore, these methods are not amenable to on-site and real-time monitoring [22], despite their high sensitivity and selectivity.

An alternative to detect emerging pollutants is to employ electrochemical sensors, which offer several advantages. These sensors can be fabricated at relatively low-cost, and feature a fast response, high sensitivity, and possibility of miniaturization to use small sample volumes (of the order of microliters). The photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensors, in particular, may provide even higher sensitivities than ordinary electrochemical sensors because their signal/noise ratios are increased with the low background signal due to the distinction between the signal input (light source) and signal response (photocurrent) [23]. The semiconductors utilized in photoelectrochemistry include the inorganic TiO2, CdSe, TeCd and BiVO4, and organic graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [24–26]. The main drawbacks associated with these semiconductors are their toxicity and non-biodegradability [27], in addition to the difficulties in reaching large-scale production as they require long purification processes and precise size control [28]. Compared to the latter materials, carbon spherical shells (CSS) are clearly advantageous. CSS are non-toxic and can be synthesized with inexpensive hydrothermal routes that provide homogeneous, large surface area particles [27,28–34]. Despite these advantages, CSS have not been used in photoelectroanalysis, to the best of our knowledge. Herein, we employ CSS to functionalize screen-printed carbon electrodes to produce a PEC sensor. A circuit board integrating the functions of light source and electrochemical workstation was developed in house to perform light excitation and analyze the photocurrent. The sensor was optimized to determine HQ with high sensitivity, including in tap water.

Potassium chloride (≥ 99.0%), L -proline (≥ 99%), β-estradiol (≥ 98%), thiamine – vitamin B1 (analytical grade), diuron (98%), pentachlorophenol (98%) and D -glucose (ACS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Salicylic acid (analytical grade) and L -ascorbic acid (analytical grade) were acquired from Synth, Brazil. Hydrochlorothiazide (≥ 99%) was purchased in a local drugstore. Sodium sulfate anhydrous (ACS, J. T. Baker, USA), sodium oxalate (analytical grade, Merck, Germany) and ethanol (analytical grade,) were purchased from J. T. Baker (USA), Merck (Germany) and Êxodo Científica (Brazil), respectively. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) were prepared with sodium phosphate dibasic (≥ 99.0%, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) and potassium phosphate monobasic (≥ 99.0%, Sigma, USA), with sodium and potassium cations being mixed due to the similarity with the sample composition. Screen-printed electrodes were made with carbon conductive ink (EDAG 423SS E&C – Henkel, Germany) while pseudo-reference electrodes were made with silver conductive paste (code 90701 – Ticon, Brazil). All aqueous solutions were prepared with high-purity water (resistivity > 18 MΩ cm) from a Nanopure Ultrapurification System Barnstead (Thermo Scientific, USA). Aqueous solutions of hydroquinone (≥ 99.5%, Merck, Germany) were prepared and used in the same day to avoid degradation.

Photoelectrochemical experiments were carried out using a potentiostat/galvanostat PGSTAT 302N (Ecochemie, Netherlands) coupled to a computer and controlled by software NOVA 2.1.3 (Metrohm, Switzerland). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data were acquired with a PGSTAT 302 system, controlled by FRA2 software, in the frequency range between 0.1 Hz and 100 kHz with amplitude of 10 mV and under open circuit potential (OCP) conditions in 0.1 mol L–1 KCl solution containing 5.0 mmol L–1 K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]. The light source was an UV LED (9 W) operated by a lab made controller board (UNO) integrated to a relay and programed by Arduino software. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained with a LEO 440 microscope model 7060 (Zeiss-Leica, Germany) operating at 20 kV electron beam equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy module. ImageJ 1.53 software was used to estimate the diameter distribution of CSS (National Institute of Health, USA). Diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) spectrum was obtained with UV-Vis spectrophotometer, UV-2600 model (Shimadzu, Japan), within the wavelength range from 185 to 900 nm, connected to a computer and managed by software UVProbe 2.7 (Shimadzu, Japan). A high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) JEOL, JEM2100 model, operating at 200 kV was used for the morphological analysis of CSS, which were ultrasonicated for 2 h in water, and the suspension was dropped on carbon–coated copper grids. The chemical surface composition of CSS was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a conventional spectrometer (ScientaOmicron ESCA+) and high-performance hemispheric analyzer (EAC2000) with monochromatic Al Kα (hn = 1486.6 eV) radiation as the excitation source.

CSS were prepared using the hydrothermal synthesis [34] by dispersing 6.5 g glucose in 72 mL ultrapure water for 15 min under vigorous agitation. Then, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave (100 mL), heated until 180 ºC at a rate of 5 ºC min–1 and kept for 5 h. The product from the synthesis was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min and washed with water, centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min followed by washing with ethanol and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. The wash/centrifugation cycle was repeated three times. The precipitate was dried at 90 ºC overnight.

The patterns of SPCE were designed in AutoCAD 2018 software (Autodesk, San Rafael, CA) and were transferred to the 77-mesh polyester stencil. The carbon conductive ink was transferred directly to the surface of a polyester substrate using a polyurethane squeegee (77 sh) followed by curing at 90 ºC for 30 min to evaporate the solvent from the ink. The pseudo-reference electrodes were made using silver conductive ink (Ag/AgCl) and cured at 90 ºC for 30 min [35,36]. The electrochemical system comprised three electrodes, viz. a carbon counter electrode, a working and a pseudo-reference electrodes, as shown in Fig. 1. The SPCEs were pretreated in 0.5 mol L–1 H2SO4 solution using cyclic voltammetry in a potential range from –2.5 to + 2.5 V at 100 mV s–1 (two scans) to remove non-conductive compounds and impurities from the carbon ink. This increases the number of electroactive sites such as quinone, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups deposited on the carbon surface [35,37]. The electrodes were washed with deionized water. Then, 6 µL CSS (2.5, 5.0, 7.5 and 10 mg mL–1) suspension was dropped on the working electrode surface with geometric area 0.12 cm2 and functionalized sensor was stored in a desiccator for drying and storage until use.

The concentration of CSS suspension, light source, potential applied and supporting electrolyte were studied to optimize HQ detection. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated using LOD = a + 3S
y/x , in which a is the intercept and S
y/x is the standard deviation of the regression line [38]. HQ determinations were carried out in tap water samples collected in São Carlos (Sao Paulo, Brazil) with geographical coordinates 22°0′19.3″S47°53′51.8″W. Sodium oxalate solid used to optimize the supporting electrolyte was dissolved directly in the tap water. The pH was adjusted with sodium hydroxide to pH 7.2 and then the samples were fortified with three concentrations of HQ (6.0, 13.0 and 20.0 µmol L–1). No pretreatment was required for the tap water samples. The photocurrent response was obtained after sample analysis and the recovery value was estimated by interpolation on the calibration curves. The reproducibility was evaluated with three SPCE in a solution containing 13.0 µmol L–1 of HQ. The experiments were carried out in the same day (intraday, n = 5) and in different days (inter-day, n = 3). Selectivity was studied by comparing the current values for 13.0 µmol L–1 HQ in the absence and in the presence of possible interferents. All electrochemical measurements were made by dropping 200 µL solutions on the electrode, and each device was used only once to avoid adsorption effects.

The schematic representation of the detection system in Fig. 1A features the working electrode illuminated with a light source controlled via an Arduino circuit board connected to the laptop, which acts as display terminal and control center for PEC sensing. The working principle is depicted in Fig. 1B where CSS has the role of a photoactive material to produce photocurrents when illuminated as electrons from the valence band (VB) are photoexcited to the conduction band (CB). Hydroquinone reacts with the holes generated by the light source, and electrons are donated to VB (CSS), thus hindering electron-hole recombination [39]. In this step, HQ is oxidized and the photocurrent response is recorded as the PEC sensing response. The band gap energy (Eg) of CSS was estimated by UV–Vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectroscopy using the Kubelka–Munk [40–43] relationship through the following equations F(Rd) = α = (1 – Rd)2/2 Rd and (αhv) = B (hv – Eg) where F(Rd) is the Kubelka–Munk function, Rd is the diffuse reflectance at a given hν, α is the absorption coefficient, h is the Planck constant (4.13 × 10−15 eV s), ν is the frequency (s−1) and σ is 2 or 2/3 for direct transition (allowed and forbidden, respectively) and 1/2 or 1/3 for indirect transition (allowed and forbidden, respectively) [41–43]. Eg was estimated as 1.3 eV by extrapolating the linear range of the Tauc plot, i.e. (αhν)1/2 vs. hν, in Fig. 1 C. The band gap in graphene and other carbon-based materials may vary between 0 and 2.7 eV as listed in Table 1. This wide range of variation is due to the effects of size, shape and chemical composition of functional groups on Eg [44–46].

The low Eg of 1.3 eV for CSS should be attributed to their spherical, 150 nm diameter shape, in addition to the presence of carbonyl, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [47,48]. The latter hypothesis is put forward based on the literature for other carbon-based nanomaterials as the optoelectronic properties of CSS have not been determined in detail. In order to provide at least some qualitative tests for the hypothesis, we performed XPS and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements as the energy levels in carbon nanomaterials depend on the hybridization of carbons atoms and on functional groups. The survey XPS spectrum for CSS in Fig. 2A indicates the predominance of carbon (81.95%) and oxygen (18.05%) with binding energies of 285 and 532.5 eV, respectively [31]. The deconvolution of the spectrum for C1S in Fig. 2B leads to five components with binding energies at 283.5 and 284.5 eV due to single and double bonds between carbon atoms, and components with binding energy at 285.5, 286.5 and 287.5 eV associated with C–OH (24%), CO (10%) and HO–CO (7%) bonds, respectively. The O1S spectrum in Fig. 2C indicates three components with binding energies at 531 and 532 eV related to the single and double bonds between carbon and oxygen atoms, respectively, and at 533.4 eV due to water molecules adsorbed on the CSS. Hence, from the XPS results one conclude that the CSS surface is rich in oxygenated groups. The adsorption of CSS on SPCE affected the electrical properties according to the Nyquist diagrams in Fig. 2D, which were fitted with a Randle’s modified equivalent circuit [Rs(CPE[RctZW])], where Rs is the solution resistance, Rct is the charge transfer resistance, CPE is a constant phase element and ZW is the Warburg impedance. The apparent heterogeneous electron rate constant was determined using Kapp = RT/F2RctCA, where F is the Faraday constant (96,485.34 C mol−1), C is the probe redox concentration in solution (5.0 mmol L−1), R is the gas constant (8.3145 J K−1 mol−1), T is the temperature (298 K), A is the geometric area (0.124 cm2) and Rct was obtained by fitting the data. Kapp was 0.41 and 0.31 µcm s−1 for bare SPCE and SPCE/CSS, respectively. Rct increased from 1.03 for bare SPCE to 1.39 kΩ for SPCE/CSS.

The XPS analysis for CSS pointed to 50.4% sp3 and 46.4% sp2, where the significant amount of sp2 should decrease Eg while the high sp3 proportion hinders CSS from being a conductor. This conclusion is based on the finding that carbon thin films had their band gap decreasing from 1.3 to 0.68 eV with the increase of amount of sp2 [49] attributed to their π interactions [50]. The results from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy are consistent with the conclusion, as the incorporation of CSS induced an increase in charge-transfer resistance. The increase was expected for non-conducting materials, but it was not as large as it would have been for an insulator.

Several parameters and conditions were varied to obtain optimized performance in detecting HQ. Changes were made in the CSS amount in the sensing unit, in the supporting electrolyte, in the potential applied in chronoamperometry, and illumination, which was made with white and UV light. The change in current (∆I) induced by 100 µmol L–1 HQ increased with the amount of CSS employed in the working electrode, as indicated in Fig. 3A and Fig. 3B. Since the photocurrent signal tends to level off between 5 and 10 mg mL–1, one may assume that thicker CSS film may hinder electron transfer [53]. Fig. 3C shows that both white LED and UV LED were efficient to generate photocurrent, which was expected owing to the 1.3 eV band gap for CSS. Nevertheless, the photocurrent response with UV LED was 1.3 fold the value for the white LED because of the additional photogenerated charge carriers at the CSS/solution interface [54]. The applied potential between 0.1 and 0.4 V in chronoamperometry has small effect on the photocurrent according to Fig. 3D and Fig. 3E, as expected from the literature [55]. Phosphate-buffer, sodium sulfate, sodium oxalate and potassium chloride solutions were evaluated as supporting electrolyte with pH ~ 7 in Fig. 3F. The highest photocurrent was observed with phosphate-buffer solution, but the signals were unstable, decreasing after each on-off cycle. With sodium oxalate solution, high and stable photocurrents were measured, thus indicating that the holes can be captured by oxalate with a higher amount of electrons collected at the auxiliary electrode [54,56]. In view of the results above, the experimental conditions selected for the subsequent experiments were: 5.0 mg mL–1 CSS, UV LED, potential applied 0.2 V and sodium oxalate as supporting electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized experimental conditions, the photocurrent response in Fig. 4A increased with HQ concentration from 3 to 23 µmol L−1. All photocurrents responses are positive (anodic) indicating that CSS behaves a n-type semiconductor, as reported in the literature [57–59]. The linear dependence in Fig. 4B can be fitted with the regression equation ∆I [µA] = 0.069 ( ± 0.004) Chydroquinone [µmol L−1] – 0.07 ( ± 0.04), with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.994. The detection limit was estimated as 2.7 µmol L−1 using the Miller & Miller method [60,61]. The device functionalized with CSS was stable for 45 days as reported by our group [62]. The PEC sensor for HQ sensing is competitive compared with previous platforms listed in Table 2, in addition to being advantageous due to the use of inexpensive and non-toxic carbonaceous nanomaterial in a green-synthesis methodology as semiconductor (each device costs less than US$ 0.10). Indeed, to be used in similar sensors, carbon nanotubes (SWCNT and MWCNT), graphene and derivatives need to be functionalized with concentrated acids, oxidative solutions and reducing reagents (for graphene). These procedures lead to chemical waste and low synthesis yield per gram of material, thus increasing cost. Furthermore, sensing layers based on transition metal semiconductors require a sintering step and are more toxic than CSS whose synthesis uses only water and glucose. Devices fabricated with platinum or gold nanoparticles are expensive due to the use of noble metals and require reducing agents.

The reproducibility of the PEC sensor was tested with intra-day and inter-day measurements in a solution containing 13.0 µmol L–1 HQ using five and three freshly prepared electrodes, respectively. The photocurrent responses are shown in Fig. 5A and Fig. 5B, where the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 8.8% and 12%, respectively, which are acceptable for the analysis of emerging pollutants. The repeatability with the same device is shown in Fig. 5C in which the photocurrent signal decreases between the first and fifth measurement. Thus, the devices should be used only once. The reproducibility can be improved with an automatic system to obtain fully-printed devices using large-scale production techniques, such as roll-to-roll processing [70].

Selectivity of the PEC sensor was evaluated with different classes of substances found in real water samples, including drugs (hydrochlorothiazide), vitamins (ascorbic acid and thiamine), hormones (β-estradiol), pesticides (diuron), amino acids ( L -proline) and pentachlorophenol. The photocurrents in Fig. 6 indicate a similar value for HQ alone (black) and in the presence of interferents (colored bars). The largest interferences were observed for ascorbic acid (+17.7%) and thiamine (–16.1%), while for the other compounds there were only small changes. The selectivity could be enhanced using a potential lower than 0.2 V to collect the photocurrent response or decorate the CSS with metallic nanoparticles, thin films of transition metals, carbon dots and quantum dots of graphene.

The PEC sensor was used to determine HQ in fortified tap water samples at three concentrations (6.0, 13.0 and 20.0 µmol L–1) to simulate contaminated water. The average recovery in Table 3 ranged from 91.5% to 107%. The satisfactory results demonstrate that the PEC sensor has a high accuracy and potential for HQ sensing in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electronic microscopy of PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: CSS have a uniform spherical morphology with particles in interfacial contact, as in the SEM images in Fig. 7A. The distribution histogram in Fig. 7B indicates a narrow size distribution from 90 to 230 nm with average diameter 150 ± 30 nm. The EDX spectra in Fig. 7C depict only carbon and oxygen, in addition to gold signals from metallization for SEM analysis indicating the absence of contaminants. The SEM images in Fig. 7D of bare SPCE show carbon graphitic flakes and carbon nanoballs of carbon black from the carbon ink formulation while Fig. 7E shows the working electrode decorated with CSS. The HR-TEM images of CSS on copper grids in Fig. 7F reveal nanosphere-shaped particles with a narrow size distribution of ca. ∼160 nm.

We exploited CSS as the sensing layer in photoelectrochemical device to detect HQ in tap water samples. CSS with average diameter of 150 nm were synthesized using a green methodology with glucose and water as precursors. The CSS band gap of 1.3 eV was effective for photoelectroanalysis, particularly for the determination of phenolic compounds. In comparison to carbon nanotubes (MWCNT and SWCNT), graphene and derivatives, CSS are advantageous owing to their high surface area, chemical and physical stability, and because they can be obtained using a greener, simple synthesis methods. The PEC sensor showed high sensitivity, selectivity, and reproducibility with satisfactory percentage of HQ recovery in real water samples. Since the PEC platform with CSS is entirely generic, further applications and improvements can be made by modifying CSS with metal nanoparticles, quantum dots or nanostructured films. This type of modification could help increase the current signals to attain optimized analytical performance. The complete detection system can be miniaturized to allow for portability and on-site analysis, for instance with a 3D-printed device containing a dark box, light source and connections to the potentiostat [72]. Furthermore, the results reported here amount to a demonstration that PEC sensors made with CSS can be utilized in the routine analysis of emerging pollutants.

Lucas V. L. Martoni: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Nathalia O. Gomes: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Thiago M. Prado: Methodology, Data curation. Marcelo L. Calegaro: Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization. Osvaldo N. Oliveira Jr.: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Sergio A. S. Machado: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Paulo A. Raymundo-Pereira: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financed in part by Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior – Brasil (CAPES) – Finance Code 001. The authors are thankful to São Paulo Research Foundation - FAPESP (grants 2020/09587–8, 2018/22214–6, 2019/01777–5 and 2016/01919–6), National Council of Technological and Scientific Development - CNPq (grants 140408/2019–3, 164569/2020–0, 151200/2022–0 and 423952/2018–8). The authors are especially grateful to Mr. Marcio de Paula (CAQI/IQSC/USP) for the SEM images.
